g.data-narration: Concurrent processes sharing data can lead to inconsistent results. Process synchronization ensures orderly execution when multiple processes access shared resources.
g.text: Ensuring orderly access to shared data among concurrent processes

g.data-narration: Race condition: multiple processes access and manipulate shared data concurrently. The final value depends on the order of execution. Results are unpredictable.
Producer-consumer problem: producer adds items to buffer, consumer removes. Must synchronize so consumer does not try to consume from empty buffer and vice versa.
g.text: Producer: adds items to shared buffer

g.data-narration: Critical section: code segment where shared data is accessed. Entry section requests permission. Exit section releases. Remainder section is non-critical code.
Three requirements for critical section solution. Mutual exclusion: only one process in CS. Progress: selection cannot be postponed indefinitely. Bounded waiting: limit on entries.
g.text: 1. Mutual Exclusion: one at a time

g.data-narration: Software solutions include Peterson's algorithm for two processes. Hardware solutions include test-and-set and compare-and-swap atomic instructions. Both prevent race conditions.
OS-level tools: mutex locks provide simple locking. Semaphores generalize mutex with counting. Monitors are high-level language constructs for synchronization.
Synchronization prevents race conditions on shared data. Critical sections must satisfy mutual exclusion, progress, and bounded waiting for correct concurrent execution.
g.text: Race Conditions solved by: Mutual Exclusion + Progress + Bounded Waiting
